SAP Learning Course

https://learning.sap.com/courses/creating-data-connections-for-cloud-applications-in-sap-analytics-cloud

This free course makes you able to create live and import data connections to cloud applications in SAP Analytics Cloud. There is neither practice system nor instructor in this course.

 

2 - Instructor-Led Training for Administrating SAP Analytics Cloud :

On another hand, you can also choose to be trained in a classroom by following the public course for administrators, which is delivered by an instructor with a practice system for participants :

  • SAP Analytics Cloud: Administration and Data Connectivity (SACA21)

 https://training.sap.com/course/saca21-sap-analytics-cloud-administration-and-data-connectivity-remoteclassroom-061-fr-en?

This course provides the knowledge required to administer your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant and connect to various SAP and non-SAP data sources. It features practical methods for applying the concepts and plenty of hands-on exercises. Duration for this public course is 5 days.

Regards,
Pierre LANSALOT-BASOU
SAP instructor - SAP France

entry.title: From Insights to Foresight: Elevating Analytics with SAP Analytics Cloud, Joule, BTP and Perplexity
entry.content:
From Insights to Foresight: Elevating Analytics with
SAP Analytics Cloud, Joule, BTP and Perplexity

Introduction

We’ve all been there: you have the internal sales figures, but you’re missing the "why" from the outside world. Is a raw material shortage in Asia spiking your costs? Is a competitor’s new product launch affecting your pipeline?

In today’s fast-paced business environment, having data isn't enough; you need the right data at the right time, regardless of where it lives. Whether it’s a row in a database, a paragraph in a PDF, or a trending market report on the web, the goal is to provide a unified experience.

This post explores how we are extending the standard capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud using the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and AI Foundation to create massive value. We will make use of a demo scenario in which our famous BestRunBikes company is facing significant declines in the racing bike segment.

 

1. The Core: Master Your Structured Data with Analytical Insights

The journey begins with making structured data accessible to everyone, not just analysts. With Analytical Insights in Joule (released in Q2 2025), users can interact with their business data using natural language. With Joule in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) we bring this analytical power to the fingertips of our SAC users (planned GA see below).

Example: At the Homescreen of SAC I recognize that my order value faces a significant decrease. We jump into the Metrics list to dive deeper. The Smart Insights (as built-in embedded ai capability) indicates that the racing product type is causing the issue. I want to understand which products belong to that type, so i ask Joule to "show me the product sales for racing" which results in an immediate visual chart generated by SAC.

 

Value:

  • Democratized Access: Analytics are available directly from line-of-business applications, saving time and training costs.
  • Powered by "Just Ask": This feature is enabled by SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and its "just ask" functionality.
  • Deep Integration: It supports both SAC acquired models and SAP Datasphere models, ensuring data-access controls and authorizations remain intact.

Note: The demo scenario shows Joule in SAC, which is a feature that is not yet general available. As of date of the post, the current plan is for Controlled Release with SAC QRC2 2026 & a planned GA with QRC3 2026. The timelines are subject to change as per the usual disclaimers. The experience of Joule itself though can already be achieved with Joule being deployed somewhere else like e.g. Successfactors, S4, Workzone,...

 

2. The Context: Grounding AI in Your Company’s Wisdom

The first step in extending Joule is giving it access to your "dark data"—the unstructured information living in PDFs, images & more. Using the SAP BTP Document Grounding service, we can link Joule to internal sources like SharePoint or Workzone.

Example: In our demo we want to understand if there are certain reasons for the decline in racing bikes. We know that we repeatedly buy market research that lives as pdf in our sharepoint. We understand that there is reduced consumer spending and increased safety concerns from the customers. We could now reach out to the product manager for racing bikes to discuss this.

 

Value:

  • Unstructured Data Integration: Access internal information stored in documents on platforms like SharePoint or Workzone.
  • Internal Knowledge Base: Users can query for internal guidelines, KPI definitions, or existing market research documents.
  • Reliable Foundation: This is an official BTP service, designed to provide "grounding" for AI, ensuring responses are based on your actual business documents.

Have a look at our AI Discovery Center to learn more about this service.

 

3. The Vision: Global Intelligence with Perplexity Integration

The final frontier is connecting your internal world with the external one. By building a custom Joule Skill in Joule Studio, we can tap into the power of Perplexity for real-time web information.

Example: We want to have the latest informations / data around the bike market and specifically look into the european market. We can also ask for certain market projections. With the help of the perplexity skill we can get this kind of information right at the fingertips of the enduser. And now imagine to use this directly as input for simulation & planning!

 

The Architecture Stack

To bring Perplexity into the Joule interface, we utilize three core pillars of the SAP Business Technology Platform:

  • Joule Studio: This is where the Custom Skill is defined and configured. It acts as the front-end logic that tells Joule when to trigger an external search.
  • Cloud Foundry (The CAP Wrapper): We use a Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model wrapper running on Cloud Foundry. This layer handles the API logic, ensuring the data is formatted correctly between the SAP ecosystem and the external provider. Thanks to vibe coding this was minimal effort.
  • AI Foundation (SAP AI Core): The heavy lifting of the AI communication happens here. We use SAP AI Core to securely query Perplexity, providing a robust and governed gateway to the web.

 

Value:

  • External Market Reach: Extend Joule’s information reach to include external market data, projections, and raw material price developments.
  • Technical Synergy: This setup uses SAP AI Core to query Perplexity, acting as a bridge between the enterprise and the live web. It ensures that external queries are handled with enterprise-grade security.
  • Future-Ready: While this can be built today as a custom skill, it is already on the roadmap to become a standard capability of Joule.

 

Conclusion: The Power of an Integrated Platform

By combining the Business Data Cloud (SAC and Datasphere) with the SAP BTP AI Foundation, we aren't just building tools; we are building an intelligent ecosystem. We are turning Joule from an analytical chatbot into a comprehensive business assistant that knows your data, your documents, and the world at large. 

 

Cheers,

Tobias

entry.title: Dynamic Opening and Closing Balance Calculation in SAP Analytics Cloud Using Script Logic
entry.content:
Introduction 

In SAP Analytics Cloud particularly in financial and treasury reporting often we have a most common requirement to display opening and closing balance based on selected period.

Mostly the requirement will be done by manually adjusting the reporting period or by creating multiple static filters , but in this blog we are going to tackle the requirement by using SAC scripting and input controls. By Filtering the periods before and up to the selected period , SAC can automatically calculate the opening and closing balance and display it in the dashboard.

This blog explains how SAC scripting is used to dynamically show the opening and closing balances based on selected period.

 Business Scenario

The requirement is that the users should view treasury balances based on the selected period. The exact scenario described as following:

When a user chooses a period from dropdown 

  • It should display opening balance ie., it should consider all periods before the user selected period 
  •  It should display closing balance ie., it should consider all periods up to the selected period and 
  •  All other charts should display values only for that selected period.

Approach Used:

SAC scripting is used in OnInitialization event to retrieve selected period and apply necessary changes on the charts to display opening and closing balances based on selected period when the dashboard is loading.

Implementation

The code  written inside the OnInitialization event ensures that filtering happens automatically in the backend.

Step 1 – Dropdown Selection and OnInitialization logic

When the user selects a period from the dropdown, the script retrieves the selected value and applies filters to the charts accordingly.The selected key gets stored in a variable and applied as a filter.

Scriptvariable_Getkey=Dropdown_1.getSelectedKey();
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey);

The script then retrieves all members available in the POSTING_YEAR dimension and converts string to numbers to achieve proper comparison.

Scriptvariable_Memberinfo=Chart_2.getDataSource().getMembers("POSTING_YEAR");
Scriptvariable_Number=ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Getkey);

In this step the script identifies all the periods before the selected period to and applies the filtered periods on Chart_2 to retrieve Opening balance.

for (var i = 0; i < Scriptvariable_Memberinfo.length; i++) {
 var year = ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[i].id); 
if (year < Scriptvariable_Number) { Scriptvariable_Filter.push(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[i].id); 
} 
}
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Filter);

To calculate closing balance in this step the script retrieves all the period up to the selected period and applies the filtered period on Chart_3.

for (var l = 0; l < Scriptvariable_Memberinfo.length; l++) { 
var year1 = ConvertUtils.stringToNumber(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[l].id); 
if (year1 <= Scriptvariable_Number) { Scriptvariable_Filter1.push(Scriptvariable_Memberinfo[l].id);
 } 
}
Chart_3.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Filter1);

All other charts are filtered only for the selected period.

Chart_9.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey); 
Chart_10.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("POSTING_YEAR",Scriptvariable_Getkey);

Step 2 – Default Behaviour on Page Load

Initially when the dashboard gets loaded in dropdown it shows the current year and month will be in the dropdown to enhance usability.The below script retrieves current month and year and formats accordingly.

Scriptvariable_CurrentYear = (new Date()).getFullYear().toString(); Scriptvariable_Currentmonth=(new Date()).getMonth().toString();
ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum=ConvertUtils.stringToInteger(Scriptvariable_Currentmonth); ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum1=ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum+1; ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr=ConvertUtils.numberToString(ScriptVariable_CurrentmonthNum1);
ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr1=('0'+ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr).slice(-2);
Scriptvariable_Combine=Scriptvariable_CurrentYear+ScriptVariable_Currentmonthstr1;

 The below script ensures that users always see the recent financial data immediately after opening the dashboard.

Dropdown_1.setSelectedKey(Scriptvariable_Combine);

Conclusion:

Dynamic reporting is necessary especially for a financial dashboard where the requirement is to automatically show opening and closing balances without any manual workload . SAC scripting approach simplifies the process and improves efficiency and enhances the user experience.

Output :

 Dynamic Dropdown for Period-Based Filtering

Dynamic Balance Calculation Based on Selected Period

entry.title: Dynamic Filtering in SAC using Logged in user details to show organization level data
entry.content:
Introduction :

In SAC a common requirement is to filter data automatically based on the logged-in user. SAC makes this relatively straightforward because the logged-in User ID can be retrieved directly through scripting.

In our scenario, the requirement was slightly different users should not see only their own records, but rather all the records belonging to their organization. To achieve this requirement we need to first get the organization details of the logged in user then pass it as a filter across the story.

This blog will explain how this requirement shall be implemented using scripting in SAC and hidden helper charts.

Business requirement :

The dashboard is used by users from multiple organizations.

So when the user opens the dashboard:

  1. The user information need to be identified.
  2. Then we need to find the organization associated with the user.
  3. The dashboard should display all the records related to the users organization.
  4. User should be able to see the records related to other users of the same organization.

    Approach used:

    You may think why can’t we directly filter by user ID for this scenario, we could filter the dashboard using the user ID but doing that it would only show the data related to that particular user we won’t be able to show the records of all other users of the same organization.

    So to achieve this,

    1. We first get the logged in user ID.
    2. Retrieve the organization associated with the user.
    3. Apply that organization as filter to the dashboard.
    4. In addition to that we have also dynamically populated the dropdown based on the filtered data.

      To achieve this we shall use 2 helper charts in the story. These charts will be hidden in the dashboard and will be only used to retrieve data through scripting.

      Chart 1 - is used to retrieve the organization associated with the user.

      Chart 2 : Retrieves the user belonging to the filtered organization.

      Code :

      The code is written in the onIntialization event of the SAC story

      1. Retrieve the ID of the user and pass it as filter on Chart 1 to retrieve the relevant records.

var1 = Application.getUserInfo().id;
Chart_1.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("PR_CREATED_BY",var1);

2. After applying the filter we retrieve the result set and get the user organization.

var2 = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet();
for (var i = 0; i < var2.length; i++) {
var3.push(var2[i]["Organisation"].id);
}

3. Populate the dropdown

Dropdown_1.removeAllItems();
for (var j = 0; j < var3.length; j++) {
Dropdown_1.addItem(var3[j]);
}

4. Applying the organization filter to the table and retrieving the user based on organization.

Table_4.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Organisation", var3);
Chart_2.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Organisation",var3);
var5 = Chart_2.getDataSource().getResultSet();
for (var k = 0; k < var5.length; k++) {
var6.push(var5[k]["PR_CREATED_BY"].id);
}

Therefore through the above approach the user can see the organisation level details.

Output Scenarios :
1. When one user is associated with more than one organization all those organization will get populated in the dropdown and same will be shown in table


2. The dashboard displays all records belonging to the user’s organization, allowing users to view data created by other users within the same organization.



3. Hidden charts that are used to fetch user organization and user id details

entry.title: Beyond Row-Level Security: Implementing Widget-Level Custom Authorization in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
The Challenge: One Dashboard to Rule Them All (But Not Show Them Everything)

We’ve all been there. The Board of Directors requests a "Management Dashboard". They want a 360-degree view of the company: Production efficiency, HR turnover, Finance liquidity, and Sales KPIs all in one place.

But here is the catch: While the CEO wants to see everything, the Head of Production shouldn't be distracted by sensitive HR Payroll charts, and the Sales Lead doesn't need to see the Finance department's deep-dive debt ratios.

The Problem: Standard Row-Level Security (RLS) only filters the data inside the charts. If a user isn't authorized, they often see a "No Data" ghost-chart or a broken visualization. This ruins the Executive Experience.

The Goal: If you aren't authorized to see the data, the chart shouldn't even exist on your screen, or in other words, it should not render.

In SAC, this is achieved by bridging the gap between the User Profile and the Canvas Scripting Engine. We aren't just securing data; we are orchestrating the user interface.

Prerequisites for Success

Before we dive into the "How-To," ensure you have the following in place:

  1. User Mapping Table: A central source (could be an acquired CSV or an import data from any system) that maps User IDs to their respective Functional Areas/KPIs.

  2. Consistent Naming Conventions: Ensure your charts are named logically in the Outline (e.g., Chart_Inv_Units, Chart_Fin_Revenue, Chart_Units_Sold) to make your scripts maintainable.
  3. Dashboard with KPIs visualized: Have a dashboard ready with some charts in it. (In this example., Revenue, Units Sold, and Units in the Inventory are the numeric KPI tiles.)

The image shows you the charts in the dashboard with their respective names.

Note: Having all the Charts/Widgets in a flow layout panel helps the flexibility of adjusting the position of the widgets based on the visibility of other widgets.

The Solution: Step-by-Step Implementation

1. Defining the Authorization Matrix

Start by creating a specialized model that defines who sees what. This acts as our "Source of Truth" for the UI. 

In this use case, I use Excel as an authorization matrix.

user_idkpi_tiledummy
GE285115rev1
GE283973uni1
GE283973invuni1
GE285115uni1

From the table, we get to know that the user GE285115 can see only "rev" and "uni" kpis, but not the "invuni". And the user GE283973 can see "uni" and "invuni" kpis, but not the "rev" kpi.

Now, using this table from Excel or from any system, create an import model that looks exactly like this.

2. The onInitialization Scripting Magic

The heart of this solution lies in the onInitialization event. When the dashboard loads, we don't wait for the user to click anything. We programmatically "audit" the user and hide unauthorized components.

Before implementing the code in the onInitialization, create a table using the Auth model that was created in the previous step with the following fields in it, and make it hidden by default.

Here's the code for our example:

Application.showBusyIndicator("Applying Visual level authorization...");
var user_id = Application.getUserInfo();
var i = 0;
Table_1.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("user_id",user_id.id);
var result = Table_1.getDataSource().getResultSet();
console.log(result);
for(i=0;i<result.length;i++){
	if(result[i]["kpi_tile"].id==="rev")
	{
		Chart_Fin_Revenue.setVisible(true);
	}
	else if(result[i]["kpi_tile"].id==="uni")
	{
		Chart_Units_Sold.setVisible(true);
	}
	else if(result[i]["kpi_tile"].id==="invuni")
	{
		Chart_Inv_Units.setVisible(true);
	} // Add else if incase if you have additional kpis
}
Application.hideBusyIndicator();

And make the default visibility of each chart hidden, as shown below.

3. Testing

Logged in as GE283973 (Assumption: This user should see only "Units Sold" and "Units in Inventory" KPI)

Logged in as GE285115 (Assumption: This user should see only "Units Sold" and "Revenue" KPI)

Lifecycle & Governance:

Maintaining your security matrix shouldn’t be a headache. Whether you’re uploading a flat file or scheduling a job from a source system to the auth model, the admin or the model owner has to maintain it. Always set your import method to "Clean and replace selected version of data." This ensures that as your organizational structure evolves, your dashboard security reflects the current reality, not yesterday’s permissions.

Why this matters for the Enterprise

By implementing Visualisation Level Security, you provide:

  • Reduced Cognitive Load: Executives only see what is actionable for them.
  • Clean Authorization: Instead of showing 'No Data' errors or broken charts, the UI dynamically hides unauthorized KPIs, maintaining a professional and secure dashboard layout.
  • Scalability: You maintain one dashboard instead of five different versions for five different departments.

Pro tip: One can pause the refresh for the widgets that are invisible to the users to enhance the dashboard's performance.

I hope this guide helps you elevate your next SAC project from a standard report to a premium executive experience. Have you run into similar challenges with UI-level security? If you have questions or hit a snag during your implementation, feel free to drop a comment below. I’d love to troubleshoot with you and hear your thoughts!

Happy Learning!!!

Vijay Bhaskaran

entry.title: SAC Story Versioning— Ability to save different versions of Story inside a single SAC story !!
entry.content:
Dear SAC Expert,

In this article, I would like to share information about a newly released feature of SAC :  Story Versioning—introduced in the Q1 2026 (2026.02) release. 

Based on my experience of working over 20 years with various data visualization tools, I believe this is a single feature that indicates that SAC is far more matured tool as compared to its peers and SAC (SAP) leadership's vision is way ahead of its peers.

Lets deep dive into it.

Story Versioning? : 

  • This feature lets you Create, manage, and restore up to 10 major versions of a story, with just a few clicks.
  • Opens older versions in read‑only mode, restore them, or save them as new/active versions.

If you want to save different versions of a SAC Story then once you update any story with your changes, go to Files -> Save a New Version, this allows users to save up to 10 versions of a SAC story inside the same file name.

 

Once you hit "Save as New Version" a pop up is shown where in you can mention any comments/text/identifier/ticket# related to changes done to original story 

Interestingly, once you have more than one version saved for a given story, you may notice that "File Versions" icon gets enabled and you can see all of the versions saved for the story along with "Comments/Text" which were entered while saving the new version of the story :

 

Great !! Now you can keep on adding more and more versions to the same SAC story file . For instance, I have created another version of story after making changes to PnL tab, 

 

Note that the "Current Version" is now updated to "Version 2", as Version 2 is the most recently saved version of the story.

Now, if you think you don't need Version 2 as the active (useful version), then you can go back to Version 1 by clicking "Open" and later "Restore" just below "Version 1" option on left side panel.

 

SAC is kind enough to warn you with, implications of "Restoring" an older version : 

 

If you still want to go ahead and restore, you would notice that now current version is marked as "Version 1" which is what you aimed for : 
 

Great, so overall, this works exactly as desired. Absolutely no flaws are observed!! 🙂

Can you migrate story versions from one system (DEV) to QA / PROD?

If you want to transport the stories having multiple versions,  you are going to find that  : 

  • SAC does NOT migrate all story versions when you transport a story from DEV → QA → PROD.

  • Only the current active story version is transported. The other (older) 9 versions are NOT included in the export package.

This happens because, Story versions are internal snapshots embedded inside the story object. ( i.e. 

They are not standalone repository objects) and Transport/export only includes the story file (one object), not its internal design history. Thus, SAC’s content export works strictly by GUIDs (story object GUID → dependencies), only the active version is included in export.
 
Let me explain this in a bit more technical depth: 

SAP’s story versioning is similar to:

  • Git checkpoints inside a single file
  • Major save points stored as JSON snapshots INSIDE the story

These versions do not exist as separate objects in:

  • Files
  • Public folder
  • Workspace
  • Repository
  • Export package

Therefore, transport has no object to map or migrate. Story versions are not objects.

Another way to understand this can be : Story Versioning is Dev-only Design Feature, Not a Lifecycle Feature

Lets understand it with a real-life example : lets assume that I have created 3 versions of SAC story in DEV , now I want to migrate the story, say from Dev to QA. The questions arise : Will all 3 versions get migrated? if yes, how and if no, why?

The findings are going to be  :

In DEV:

  • Story: “Sales Story”
  • Version 1: Draft layout
  • Version 2: Improved layout
  • Version 3: Final version (active one)

After export → import to QA:

Only the currently active version (Version 3) appears in QA.

Versions 1 & 2 vanish — SAC never exported them.

 Hope, this clarifies a lot of questions with regards to SAC Story Versioning.

Happy Learning!

Cheers!

~ Ashish

entry.title: Dynamically filter your SAC Story widgets for a BW Query
entry.content:
As my BW Live model in the practice system (if you want to do this on your own jump to the last section of this artice) has data only between the years 2015 and 2017, I need to use an offset between 9 and 11 years, so I created a dynamic time filtering in SAP Analytics Cloud Optimized Story to use auto-current month (YYYYMM) with a 9–11 year slider. This is what would be needed if you go against a BW datasource.

What you’ll build

  • Compute the current month in YYYYMM in script.
  • Apply that month as a filter to both a table and a chart via the Optimized Story scripting API.
  • Add a slider that lets users jump back 9, 10, or 11 years from “now.”
  • Keep table and chart synchronized by applying the same time filter to both data sources.

Prerequisites

  • You are using the Optimized Story Experience with scripting enabled.
  • Your model’s time dimension has member IDs in YYYYMM format (for example, 202403). If not, adjust the formatter/mapping.
  • You’ve enabled technical names for widgets and dimensions so you can reference them in scripts.
  • You have a table (for example, Table_1) and a chart (Chart_1) bound to the same model.
  • You can add a scripting Slider control (Slider_YearsBack) and optional text label (Text_CurrentSelection).

Key Optimized Story scripting APIs used

  • Widget.getDataSource()
  • DataSource.setDimensionFilter(dimensionId, membersArray)
  • Slider.setMinValue(number), setMaxValue(number), setStep(number), setValue(number), getValue()
  • Event handlers: Page.onInitialization, Slider.onChange
  • Text.setText(string) [optional]

Step-by-step

Add the widgets

  • Insert a chart (Chart_1) and a table (Table_1) bound to your model.
  • Create a Script Variable SV_YearsBack
  • Insert a Slider control (Slider_YearsBack).
  • Optionally insert a Text widget (Text_1) to show the current filter.
  • Confirm the technical name of your time dimension (example: Date). You’ll use it in code.
  1. Create a Script Object helper
    Create a Script function filterWidgets and paste this script. It uses standard JavaScript plus Optimized Story scripting API calls.
// Current Date (Today)

var today = new Date();

console.log(today);



// Format dates manually for display and console

var todayMonth = today.getMonth() + 1;

var todayDay = today.getDate();

var todayYear = today.getFullYear();

var filteredYear = todayYear- SV_YearsBack;

var todayStr = todayMonth.toString() + "/" + todayDay.toString() + "/" + todayYear.toString();

console.log(todayStr);



// For current month

var todayMonthPadded = "0" + todayMonth.toString();

todayMonthPadded = todayMonthPadded.slice(-2);

var filteredYYYYMM = filteredYear.toString() + todayMonthPadded;

console.log("Filter to YYYYMM: " +filteredYYYYMM);

Chart_1.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("4SACDEMO_CP-YEAR_",filteredYYYYMM);

Table_1.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("4SACDEMO_CP-YEAR_",filteredYYYYMM);

Text_1.applyText("go back "+ ConvertUtils.numberToString(SV_YearsBack)+ " years");

 

  1. Initialize on story (or page) load
    Use the Page_1 onInitialization event to apply the initial filter, via calling the function
  2. React to slider changes
    Open the Slider_YearsBack’s onChange event and call the script again.

 

That’s it. Each time the story loads, you compute the current month and default to 9 years back. Users can switch to 10 or 11 years back, and the table and chart update in sync using DataSource.setDimensionFilter.

Notes and variations

  • If your time IDs aren’t YYYYMM: Map the computed date to your model’s IDs before calling setDimensionFilter (for example, convert to 2024.P03 for fiscal period 3).
  • Use months instead of years: Configure the slider between 108 and 132, set step to 1, and pass Slider_YearsBack.getValue() directly to offsetYYYYMM without multiplying by 12.
  • Filter a range (rolling window): Build an array of YYYYMM values from the target month up to current, then pass that array to setDimensionFilter. Be mindful of performance for long ranges.
  • Story-wide behavior: If you prefer one control to drive multiple widgets via linked analysis, set the filter on a single widget that is the driver. In Optimized Story, scripting a story-level filter is limited compared to Analytic Applications, so setting the filter on each widget’s DataSource is a reliable approach.
  • Missing members: If the computed member doesn’t exist (for example, gaps in historical data), decide whether to look up the nearest available month or clear the filter. You can handle this with a pre-check using available member lists or by showing a friendly message.

Testing checklist

  • Verify that the story defaults to “current month minus 9 years” after load.
  • Move the slider to 10 and 11; ensure both chart and table refresh.
  • Check boundary behavior in January (correctly falls back into last year).
  • Validate results against known historical periods.

Troubleshooting

  • No data after filter: Ensure the computed YYYYMM exists as a member ID. Adjust the formatter or map to the model’s ID scheme.
  • Filter not applied: Confirm both widgets are bound to the same model and that you’re calling getDataSource().setDimensionFilter on each widget.
  • Timezone differences: The “current month” uses the runtime environment’s clock. If you must pin to a specific timezone or fiscal period, expose a parameter or compute from a central date reference in your data.

Tips to implement in the learning hub’s practice system (needs a subscription)

To implement this in the practice system in the learning hub. You can use the system for the courses SACE11, SACS21, or SACM21. Before you start  create a BW live model and use the connection A4H, with the BW Query P00Q_BW_Q_VAR_HIER.

If you have other ideas to implement specific things in SAP Analytics Cloud , reach out for me via personal message or emai michael.melters@sap.com I am always happy to be inspired by your input! Probably next Blog will be about while loops another topic that a student asked about that same course two weeks ago. 

Until next time

Michael

entry.title: Next Phase of the New Table Build Experience: Switching Existing Stories
entry.content:
With the upcoming QRC2 release in May, we are entering the next phase of the new table experience rollout, focused on guiding the designer to switch existing stories and composites to the new table build experience.

In earlier blog posts, we described the product direction of the new table build experience and announced that the new Table Builder panel has become the standard for story creation  in SAP Analytics Cloud. With recent releases, we have continued to mature this experience and close the remaining gaps compared to the previous table experience.

What Changes When You Edit an Existing Story

When you open a story ior composite n edit mode that still uses the previous table experience, you will now be prompted to switch to the new table build experience.

Prompt to switch to the new table build experience

  • If you confirm the switch, the story or composite is loaded and saved in the new table build experience and a backup version is created, allowing you to fall back if anything unexpected occurs.
  • If you cancel, nothing changes. The story or composite remains on the previous table experience, and if you save, you will not be prompted again until a future release. At any time, you can still initiate the switch manually using the table builder panel.

This approach ensures a smooth and controlled transition while giving you full flexibility over when the switch happens.

We understand that stories and composites are critical business assets. For that reason, the backup version is automatically created and available via the story version panel. Currently, you can only have up to 10 versions, so creating a backup isn't possible if you've already reached the limit.  In this case, stories switch to the new table experience without creating a backup or saving. This lets you delete a version, leave the story without saving, and retry the migration by reopening the story if needed.

Why Switch to the New Table Build Experience

If you’ve followed our earlier blogs, you’ll already be familiar with the advantages — but they are worth reiterating as you plan your migration.

Modern, Simplified Table Building

  • Alignment with the chart builder paradigm
  • Improved discoverability of dimensions, measures, and structure
  • Better alignment between design and consumption

Improved Performance and Functionality

  • Support for blended tables 
  • Improved Ranking and sorting experience
  • Foundation for future innovations

Long-Term Direction

Looking ahead, our goal is to deprecate the previous table experience. All ongoing innovation, enhancements, and optimizations are focused exclusively on the new table build experience.

We strongly recommend starting to switch existing stories now. Early adoption helps ensure a smooth transition and allows us to address any remaining edge cases before the previous experience is phased out.

Your Feedback Matters

As you begin switching stories, we encourage you to validate your key scenarios and let us know if you encounter any issues or restrictions. Your feedback continues to play a vital role in shaping the final stages of this transition and the future of tables in SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud in Business Data Cloud Achieves IBCS Re-Certification until February 2028!
entry.content:
In an era where AI increasingly generates and surfaces insights, clarity, consistency, and trust in how data is presented becomes more critical. The International Business Communication Standards (IBCS) provides a global framework to ensure business information is visualized in a way that is consistent, comparable, and universally interpretable.

We’re excited to share that SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) in Business Data Cloud (BDC) has been re-certified by IBCS until February 2028. This re-certification reflects SAP's continued commitment to:

  1. Standardized Visual Communication across ALL Analytics Experiences
  2. Reducing Cognitive Load for Consumers
  3. Enabling Faster and Confident Decision

Continuous Improvements toward IBCS Excellence

IBCS re-certification is driven by a series of targeted enhancements that improve how users interpret and interact with data, strengthening consistency, readability, and comparability across analytics experiences. As part of the February 2028 re-certification, SAP Analytics Cloud within Business Data Cloud introduces several key improvements, which we explore in more detail below.


Sort with 100% Stacked Bar Charts

We now support sorting for 100% stacked bar charts, allowing users to order categories based on values or measures. This enhancement improves data analysis by making trends and comparisons easier to interpret.

 


Grid Lines for Improved Readability

We’ve introduced Grid Lines which can be toggled on and off via the Designer in the Charts Show / Hide settings. It can help improve the readability of the Chart and make it easier to interpret data points that have their data labels hidden due to collision. There is an option to update the color of the Grid Lines via the Styling Panel.


+/- Indicators for Table Comparisons

Within Tables, Designers can now configure the +/- indicators for comparison calculations, making variances immediately distinguishable and clearly communicated at a glance.

 

Configurable Separator Line Color and Axis / Separator Line Thickness

Separator line and axis line thickness can now be controlled, and separator line color can be configured. Axis line color remains configurable, providing consistent flexibility in defining visual structure and hierarchy.


Improved Padding for Data Label Backgrounds

Refined data label background padding improves readability and ensures values remain clear and legible, even in compact visualizations.


Enhanced Axis Title Configuration

For Viewers to have better context, Designers can now add Axis Titles which reflects the name of the bound object. It helps in understanding what data is being represented on each axis.

For the Axis Title, we allow the configure of the color and position. For the X-Axis Title we support a left, center, and right alignment while for the Y-Axis we support a centre and top alignment.

 


Adjust Bar Width at Widget Level

You can now adjust the bar width at the widget level, instead of having to use Chart Scaling, giving designers greater control over the appearance of individual bar and column charts.

Setting the widget-level bar width overrides chart scaling bar width and default bar width settings for the chart. All other chart scaling settings are still applied.

 

Expanded Number Format Settings in Themes (Tables & Geo)

Extended number formatting options within themes ensure consistent representation of values across tables and geo visualizations, strengthening enterprise-wide standardization.

Why This Matters AND What Comes Next

IBCS is not just a design guideline, it is a foundation for trusted, scalable analytics. With this re-certification, SAC in BDC continues to:

  1. Deliver Consistent and Interpretable Insights
  2. Enable Consumers to Build Mental Models for Faster Decisions with Confidence 
  3. Provide a Standardized Layer for Analytics in AI-driven and Application-Embedded Scenarios

With this, it is important to:

  1. Revisit YOUR Dashboards and Align them with IBCS Best Practices
  2. Leverage these Enhancements to Standardize how Insights are Communicated Across YOUR Organization

entry.title: Need to Know - Unlocking the Power of AI in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
A practical guide to the AI capabilities already at your fingertips inside SAP Analytics Cloud (aka SAP Business Data Cloud)

Artificial Intelligence is no longer something you need to bolt onto your analytics platform — in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), it’s already embedded directly into the product. These AI capabilities are designed to help business users work faster, gain deeper insights, and make better decisions, without needing data science skills or complex setup.

The information below is a summary of the new exciting AI features inside SAP Analytics Cloud for both reporting (Business Intelligence) and planning use cases.  Clearly SAP will add more AI features , which I will add and update this blog once they appear.  At the end of this blog there is more information about how to use these features in your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant.

 

Ask questions in plain language with Just Ask

One of the biggest barriers to analytics adoption is technical complexity. Just Ask, SAP Analytics Cloud’s natural language query capability, removes that barrier.

Instead of building queries or knowing where data lives, users can simply ask questions in natural language, such as:

  • “Sales by region this quarter”
  • “Compare actuals versus forecast last year”
  • “Revenue for Germany and France by month”

SAP Analytics Cloud automatically returns answers as clear charts and tables, which can then be:

  • Added to stories
  • Exported to Excel or CSV
  • Explored further in the Data Analyzer

Why it matters:
Just Ask makes analytics accessible to everyone — not just power users — and dramatically reduces the time it takes to get answers from data.

 

Plan smarter with Predictive Planning

Planning is all about the future — and AI plays a major role here. Predictive Planning in SAP Analytics Cloud uses automated machine learning to turn historical data into accurate forecasts.

You can:

  • Train predictive models directly on your planning data
  • Generate forecasts at the right business level (for example, per product or entity)
  • Use predictions as a baseline and adjust them based on business knowledge

Why it matters:
Predictive Planning reduces bias, improves forecast accuracy, and helps planning teams react faster to change — all without manual calculation effort.

 

Understand why numbers change with Smart Insights

Charts show what happened — but often not why. Smart Insights fills that gap.

With a single click on a data point or variance, Smart Insights automatically analyzes the underlying data and produces:

  • A written explanation in plain language
  • Supporting visual breakdowns of contributing factors

These insights appear directly inside your story, exactly where you’re analyzing the data.

Why it matters:
Instead of manually drilling down and interpreting data, users get instant explanations — saving time and improving confidence in decisions.

 

Predict outcomes using Smart Predict

For more advanced predictive use cases, Smart Predict allows users to build and compare predictive models without being data scientists.

It supports three types of predictive scenarios:

  • Classification – predicting whether something will happen (for example, likelihood of churn)
  • Regression – predicting a numeric value (such as revenue or cost)
  • Time Series – forecasting future values over time

You can compare multiple models, choose the best-performing one, and apply it to new data — all through an intuitive interface.

Why it matters:
Smart Predict brings enterprise-grade prediction into the hands of business users, removing the need for external tools or complex modeling.

 

Create forecasts directly inside charts

Sometimes you just want a fast forecast without building a full predictive model. SAP Analytics Cloud makes this easy by allowing users to run forecasts directly in time series or line charts.

Available options include:

  • Automatic forecasting
  • Exponential smoothing (for seasonal patterns)
  • Linear regression

Advanced settings allow you to control forecast periods and even include additional influencing factors.

Why it matters:
This enables quick, visual forecasting during analysis — perfect for ad hoc insights and fast decision-making.

 

 

Discover hidden patterns with Smart Grouping

When working with scatterplots or bubble charts, spotting patterns can be difficult. Smart Grouping solves this by automatically clustering similar data points using AI‑based grouping.

With Smart Grouping, you can:

  • Identify natural clusters in your data
  • Reveal relationships that are hard to see manually
  • Accelerate exploratory analysis

Why it matters:
Users can uncover insights faster and more accurately, especially when dealing with complex or high‑volume datasets.

 

 

Build calculations using AI‑assisted calculations

Writing calculations can be time‑consuming — and understanding existing formulas can be just as challenging. AI‑assisted calculations simplifies both.

Users can:

  • Generate calculation formulas using natural language (“Create a margin percentage measure”)
  • Ask AI to explain existing formulas in plain English

This works directly in the calculation dialog in the Data Analyzer.

Why it matters:
Business users save time, reduce errors, and gain confidence when working with calculations — without needing deep technical knowledge.

 

Automatically summarise charts for PowerPoint

Creating presentations often involves manually summarising charts — a repetitive and time‑consuming task. With the AI‑assisted chart summary in the SAP Analytics Cloud PowerPoint add‑in, this step is automated.

The feature:

  • Generates a concise three‑bullet summary of a chart
  • Inserts it directly into PowerPoint as editable text
  • Can be regenerated whenever the data changes

Why it matters:
Users spend less time writing slide commentary and more time focusing on insights and storytelling.

 

Make comments easier to consume with AI‑assisted commenting

Comments are valuable — but large volumes of them can be hard to process. AI‑assisted commenting helps by:

  • Summarizing multiple comments
  • Aggregating comments across hierarchies
  • Translating comments into the user’s preferred language
  • Rephrasing comments to improve clarity and tone

Why it matters:
This makes collaboration more effective and ensures decision‑makers can quickly understand qualitative context alongside the numbers.

 

Speed up planning logic with AI‑assisted data actions

For planning professionals, AI‑assisted data actions and documentation reduce the effort involved in scripting and documentation.

With AI, you can:

  • Generate Advanced Formula scripts from plain‑language comments
  • Automatically create readable explanations of existing scripts
  • Generate documentation for entire data actions

Why it matters:
This improves productivity, simplifies maintenance, and supports better knowledge transfer across teams.

Simulate and model uncertainty quickly and easily using Compass

Whilst not strictly an AI feature Compass is a great feature that uses Monte Carlo simulation to to estimate the possible outcomes of uncertain business events.   Compass is an embedded feature that uses Monte Carlo simulation to model uncertainty in business drivers and show the probable range of outcomes for key KPIs, supporting risk-aware planning and scenario analysis.

With Compass :

  • Risk‑aware decision making: Runs many simulations quickly and easily to quantify the likelihood of outcomes, not just single best/worst cases. 
  • Easy scenario modeling: Enables no‑code “what‑if” simulations directly in SAC using existing driver–target relationships.   Just right click on a driver in a table, select Compass and you can start simulating the model using your drivers immediately.  It's as quick and easy as that! 
  • Improved planning confidence: Helps planners assess the impact of volatile drivers (e.g., costs, demand, inflation) across budgeting, target setting, and strategy
     

Why it matters:

Compass moves planning beyond static assumptions to a probabilistic view of the future, allowing organizations to make more confident, resilient decisions in the face of uncertainty.
 
Help about Compass can be found here https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/18850a0e13944f53aa8a8b7c094ea29e/3cc76bccb94648e4a3952029a7e9425c.html?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=LATEST 
 

 

 

More Information

Discover More - To learn more about each specific AI feature then go to the SAP Discovery Centre here https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/ai-catalog/?productId=f754af8c-a3a2-4bf7-bbd7-2face9ca4139 (the link is filtered on SAP Analytics Cloud Business AI features except Compass).  This excellent site provides information about the SAP Business AI features for all of SAP’s solutions, not just SAP Analytics Cloud.  

The site provides an overview of the specific feature including links to the help portal, business value overview of the feature, roadmap information, customer stories and more.  You can also find out whether the feature is already included in the SAP Analytics Cloud license or where you need to pay an additional cost (called AI units) to use the feature:

  • Base – means that AI Units are not currently required to use this AI offering.
  • Premium – means that AI Units are required to be purchased to use this AI offering.

Enable AI-Assisted Features - AI-assisted features are integrated to generate AI content in various areas of SAP Analytics Cloud. For example, you might want to obtain a summary of all comments in a lengthy comment thread. To enable your users to access these features, you need to go through an activation procedure. Go here to see how you enable these AI features https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/18850a0e13944f53aa8a8b7c094ea29e/8dcc1f1915b241b3a10c8e5b8a76b062.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST 

AI-Assisted Features Help - https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/18850a0e13944f53aa8a8b7c094ea29e/00ca4ae380794468ba42ea46d10a4045.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST 

SAP Community - In addition to the help portal we have an excellent community site for additional help, blogs, tricks and tips for using these features - Artificial Intelligence for Planning and Analytics https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/cloud-analytics/ai-for-planning-and-analytics 

 

Tips about using AI Features

As you embark on your AI adventure with SAP Analytics Cloud here are some useful tips to help you and your users adopt them...

  • AI is the tool, not the mission.  AI should be implemented to enhance human productivity, accelerate workflows, or solve specific problems, rather than adopting AI simply because it is the current trend.
  • Start your AI Journey by Experimenting with how AI can help your users. The features described above is AI embedded into the solution.  It’s quick and easy to activate and easy to use, meaning that you can try it out and experiment with your users without a lot of setup required.         
  • Execute over ambition.  Because this AI is quick and easy to activate and experiment with it means that you can execute on using AI with real use cases straight away.
  • Done is better than perfect.  Start and experiment "Perfect is the enemy of good"  meaning that striving for flawlessness can prevent the completion of worthwhile, functional tasks. Perfectionism leads to procrastination, inefficiency, and missed opportunities, whereas aiming for "good enough" allows for progress, implementation, and future improvements.
  • Don't boil the ocean.  Focus on a few things and execute well on these.
  • People over Technology.  You need people to adapt and adopt to the AI.  Otherwise it's useless and a waste of money.  Education and experimentation is the key to successful adoption

entry.title: Context-Driven Dashboard Experiences: Role-Based Layouts in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
What if a single dashboard could completely change depending on who’s viewing it?

While working on a recent project, I explored how scripting can be used to tailor dashboards dynamically based on user roles. Instead of maintaining multiple versions of the same story, I built a single dashboard that adapts in real time – showing only the widgets relevant to each user.

The approach is straightforward: start with a story where all widgets are hidden by default, then use a script to detect the user’s role on load and reveal the appropriate content. This can be as simple as adding a few extra charts, or as powerful as presenting an entirely different dashboard experience.

In this example, I focused on contextualisation by designing views for two distinct audiences – Sales and Marketing – each with their own KPIs.

The API Reference Guide is a great place to start and understand the scripting possibilities: View API Reference

This blog is the first in a series of exploring applied use cases in SAP Analytics Cloud. 

1. Create the Dashboard

I began by building the Sales view, which includes charts, a contextualised dashboard summary, and dynamic name and team text. Once this was in place, I hid all the containers within the page outline except for the dynamic name and team text.

Next, I created the Marketing view, following the same approach – building out the required components and then hiding all the relevant containers. At this stage, when the story is opened, the dashboard appears blank regardless of the user’s role.

Note: I made sure to label the containers (Sales1, Sales2, etc.) so it was easier to find what I wanted to show. 

 

2. Display Content Based on User Role

With the layout complete, I moved on to scripting.

 

Using the on-initialisation script, I first created a variable to capture the user’s team information. I then defined another variable to extract the specific team name. In addition, I created a global variable called “Team” within the page outline, which allows the team name to be displayed dynamically at the top of the dashboard.

 

var teams = Application.getTeamsInfo();
var teamName = teams[0].name;
Team = teams[0].name;

From there, it was simply a matter of using an if statement to control visibility – setting the appropriate elements to visible based on the user’s team. As a result, when a user logs in, they only see the content relevant to them, with the team name updating dynamically. 

if (teamName === "Sales") {
    Sales.setVisible(true);
    Sales1.setVisible(true);
    Sales2.setVisible(true);
    Sales3.setVisible(true);
    Sales4.setVisible(true);
}

if (teamName === "Marketing") {
    Marketing.setVisible(true);
    Marketing1.setVisible(true);
    Marketing2.setVisible(true);
    Marketing3.setVisible(true);
    Marketing4.setVisible(true);
}

 

There are plenty of opportunities for further customisation here. Beyond just showing or hiding charts, you can tailor buttons, apply filters, and even switch branding to create a more personalised experience.

entry.title: Season 10 of Let's Talk Data Podcast Series: A focus on the data professionals that make it happen
entry.content:
Season 10 of SAP Let’s Talk Data is built for practitioners who make data work. We’re shifting from high-level storytelling to hard-earned, technical lessons from engineers, scientists, stewards, and architects who design, operate, and scale real-world data systems. Expect deep, operational conversations about the challenges, trade-offs, and best practices that turn data into repeatable, observable deliverables.

 What we’ll dig into

We will cover topics like your data platform architecture, data engineering at scale, (master) data quality and observability, data modelling from data practitioners and partners.

  • Governance and security: privacy-preserving analytics, metadata management, access policy enforcement, cataloging, encryption, and auditability in regulated environments.
  • Organizational and delivery practices: cross-functional workflows between platform, product, and analytics teams, prioritization under competing SLAs, and techniques for reducing friction between ops and research.

Here is a look at the first episodes

  • A Day in the Life of a Data Steward — Behind the Scenes of Trusted Data

What does a day in the life of a data steward really look like? This podcast pulls back the curtain on the people who keep enterprise data trustworthy, usable, and compliant. We will discuss how these data steward navigate through the rhythms of their day—triaging data incidents, curating metadata, negotiating business definitions, enforcing access controls, and coordinating fixes across engineering, analytics, and business teams. We explore both process realities and the best practices in place to ensure trust.

  • All in on AI: SAP BDC and SAP Business AI - coming soon

Leona Hasani is an SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) expert who specializes in all things data analysis and data management for BDC. In this podcast, she will dive deep into how BDC can help a data scientist leverage all the BDC features and technologies to maximize the value. If you are a data scientist, work with one, or in general, manage some form of the data environment in your team, then this podcast will help you understand all the tools available to you when using SAP Business Data Cloud.

Join us!

If you build data pipelines, ship analytics products, steward sensitive data, or operate ML systems, this season is engineered to deliver actionable guidance you can adapt to your stack and scale.

Subscribe to follow Season 10 (on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, YouTube, and more) and continue the conversation with the Data Professionals Community
